*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  STATUS_0100  OUTPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE STATUS_0100 OUTPUT.
  SET PF-STATUS 'ZITEMS'.               "Creating buttons for items
  PERFORM CREATE_OBJECT_LIPS.           "perform for create item
ENDMODULE.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  USER_COMMAND_0100  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE USER_COMMAND_0100 INPUT.
  CASE SY-UCOMM.
    WHEN 'BACK'.
      LEAVE TO SCREEN 200.
    WHEN 'CREATE'.                    "Createing new record for items
       PERFORM CREATE_ITEMS_DATA.
    WHEN 'EDIT'.                      "Editable alv for items
       PERFORM CHANGE_ITEMS_MODE.
    WHEN 'UPDATE'.                    "Updateing the records for items
        PERFORM UPDATE_ITEMS_DATA.
    WHEN 'DELETE'.                    "Deleting the records for item tables
        PERFORM DELETE_ITEMS_DATA.
    WHEN 'SAVE'.                      "Saveing the recording from db table in item records
        PERFORM SAVE_ITEMS_DATA.
  ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  CREATE_OBJECT_LIPS
FORM CREATE_OBJECT_LIPS .
  CLEAR O_CONTAINER2.
  IF O_CONTAINER2 IS INITIAL AND O_GRID2 IS INITIAL.
    CREATE OBJECT O_CONTAINER2
      EXPORTING
        CONTAINER_NAME              = 'CONTAINER2'
      EXCEPTIONS
        CNTL_ERROR                  = 1
        CNTL_SYSTEM_ERROR           = 2
        CREATE_ERROR                = 3
        LIFETIME_ERROR              = 4
        LIFETIME_DYNPRO_DYNPRO_LINK = 5
        OTHERS                      = 6
        .
    CREATE OBJECT O_GRID2
      EXPORTING
        I_PARENT          = O_CONTAINER2
      EXCEPTIONS
        ERROR_CNTL_CREATE = 1
        ERROR_CNTL_INIT   = 2
        ERROR_CNTL_LINK   = 3
        ERROR_DP_CREATE   = 4
        OTHERS            = 5
        .
    ENDIF.
    CALL METHOD O_GRID2->SET_TABLE_FOR_FIRST_DISPLAY
      EXPORTING
        IS_LAYOUT                     = LS_LAYO1
      CHANGING
        IT_OUTTAB                     = LT_LIPS
        IT_FIELDCATALOG               = LT_FCAT1
      EXCEPTIONS
        INVALID_PARAMETER_COMBINATION = 1
        PROGRAM_ERROR                 = 2
        TOO_MANY_LINES                = 3
        OTHERS                        = 4
            .
    call method O_GRID2->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY( ).
ENDFORM.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  STATUS_0200  OUTPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE STATUS_0200 OUTPUT.
  SET PF-STATUS 'ZHEADER'.
  " Only load header data from DB once
  IF lt_likp IS INITIAL.
    SELECT vbeln
           ernam
           erdat
           vstel
           vkorg
      FROM zdb_edit_alv
      INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE lt_likp
      WHERE vbeln IN s_vbeln.
  ENDIF.
  " Only create container and grid once
  IF o_container1 IS INITIAL AND o_grid1 IS INITIAL.
    " Create custom container
    CREATE OBJECT o_container1
      EXPORTING
        container_name = 'CONTAINER1'.
    " Create ALV grid object
    CREATE OBJECT o_grid1
      EXPORTING
        i_parent = o_container1.
    " Set up field catalog and layout if needed (assuming lt_fcat and ls_layo are ready)
    CALL METHOD o_grid1->set_table_for_first_display
      EXPORTING
        i_structure_name              = 'ZDB_EDIT_ALV'
        is_layout                     = ls_layo
      CHANGING
        it_outtab                     = lt_likp
        it_fieldcatalog               = lt_fcat
      EXCEPTIONS
        invalid_parameter_combination = 1
        program_error                 = 2
        too_many_lines                = 3
        OTHERS                        = 4.
    " Set event handler for item-level data retrieval
    DATA(o_delivery) = NEW delivery( ).
    SET HANDLER o_delivery->get_data FOR o_grid1.
  ENDIF.
ENDMODULE.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  USER_COMMAND_0200  INPUT
MODULE USER_COMMAND_0200 INPUT.
 CASE SY-UCOMM.
   WHEN 'BACK'.
      LEAVE PROGRAM.                    "Back to the program
    WHEN 'CREATE'.                      "Creating new record for header data
      PERFORM CREATE_HEADER_DATA.
    WHEN 'EDIT'.                        "Editable alv for header data
      PERFORM CHANGE_MODE.
    WHEN 'UPDATE'.                      "Updateing the record for header data
       PERFORM UPDATE_HEADER_DATA.
  WHEN 'DELETE'.                      "deleteing for a record for header data
       PERFORM DELETE_HEADER_DATA.
    WHEN 'SAVE'.                        "saveing the db table for header data
       PERFORM SAVE_HEADER_DATA.
  ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  CHANGE_MODE
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM CHANGE_MODE .
   DATA : LT_INDEX_ROWS TYPE LVC_T_ROW,
         LT_ROW_NO     TYPE LVC_T_ROID,
         LS_ROW_NO     TYPE LVC_S_ROID,
         LS_STYLEROW   TYPE LVC_S_STYL,
         LT_STYLEROW   TYPE TABLE OF LVC_S_STYL.
  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->GET_SELECTED_ROWS
    IMPORTING
*     et_index_rows = lt_index_rows
      ET_ROW_NO = LT_ROW_NO.
  IF NOT LT_ROW_NO IS INITIAL.
    LOOP AT LT_LIKP INTO LS_LIKP.
      READ TABLE LT_ROW_NO INTO LS_ROW_NO WITH KEY ROW_ID = SY-TABIX.
      IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VBELN'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERDAT'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VSTEL'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VKORG'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        CLEAR: LS_LIKP-CELLTAB.
        INSERT LINES OF LT_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LS_LIKP-CELLTAB.
        MODIFY LT_LIKP FROM LS_LIKP TRANSPORTING CELLTAB.
        CLEAR: LT_STYLEROW.
      ELSE.
        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VBELN'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERDAT'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VSTEL'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VKORG'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        CLEAR: LS_LIKP-CELLTAB.
        INSERT LINES OF LT_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LS_LIKP-CELLTAB.
        MODIFY LT_LIKP FROM LS_LIKP TRANSPORTING CELLTAB.
        CLEAR: LT_STYLEROW.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDIF.

  CLEAR LT_ROW_NO.
  CLEAR LS_ROW_NO.

  IF O_GRID1->IS_READY_FOR_INPUT( ) EQ 0.
    CALL METHOD O_GRID1->SET_READY_FOR_INPUT
      EXPORTING
        I_READY_FOR_INPUT = 1.

  ELSE.
    CALL METHOD O_GRID1->SET_READY_FOR_INPUT
      EXPORTING
        I_READY_FOR_INPUT = 0.
  ENDIF.
 CALL METHOD O_GRID1->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY( ).

ENDFORM.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  CREATE_HEADER_DATA
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM CREATE_HEADER_DATA .
 DATA: LS_NEW        TYPE T_ZDB_EDIT_ALV,
        LT_STYLEROW   TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF LVC_S_STYL WITH DEFAULT KEY,
        LS_STYLEROW   TYPE LVC_S_STYL.

  CLEAR: LS_NEW, LT_STYLEROW.

  " Define editable fields

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VBELN'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERDAT'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VSTEL'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VKORG'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  " Assign celltab to new row
  LS_NEW-CELLTAB = LT_STYLEROW.

  " Insert new row at the beginning
  APPEND LS_NEW TO LT_LIKP.

  " Set grid to editable mode
  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->SET_READY_FOR_INPUT
    EXPORTING
      I_READY_FOR_INPUT = 1.

  " Refresh ALV to reflect changes
  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY.

ENDFORM.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  DELETE_HEADER_DATA
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------
FORM DELETE_HEADER_DATA .
  DATA: LT_ROWS TYPE LVC_T_ROW,
        LS_ROW  TYPE LVC_S_ROW,
        LV_IDX  TYPE SY-TABIX.
  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->GET_SELECTED_ROWS
    IMPORTING ET_INDEX_ROWS = LT_ROWS.
  LOOP AT LT_ROWS INTO LS_ROW.
    LV_IDX = LS_ROW-INDEX.
    READ TABLE LT_LIKP INDEX LV_IDX INTO LS_LIKP.
    IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
      DELETE FROM ZDB_EDIT_ALV WHERE VBELN = LS_LIKP-VBELN and ERNAM = LS_LIKP-ERNAM.
      DELETE LT_LIKP INDEX LV_IDX.
    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.
  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY.
  COMMIT WORK.
  MESSAGE 'Selected header(s) deleted' TYPE 'S'.

ENDFORM.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  SAVE_HEADER_DATA
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
TYPES: BEGIN OF TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV_FLAT,
         VBELN TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV-VBELN,
         ERNAM TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV-ERNAM,
         ERDAT TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV-ERDAT,
         VSTEL TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV-VSTEL,
         VKORG TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV-VKORG,
       END OF TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV_FLAT.

DATA: LS_DB_ALV_FLAT TYPE TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV_FLAT.

FORM SAVE_HEADER_DATA .

DATA: LS_DB_ALV_FLAT TYPE TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV_FLAT,
        LV_VBELN       TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV-VBELN,
        LV_SKIPPED     TYPE I VALUE 0,
        LT_LIKP_CLEAN  TYPE TABLE OF T_ZDB_EDIT_ALV,
        LS_LIKP_CLEAN  TYPE T_ZDB_EDIT_ALV,
        LV_SAVED_ROWS  TYPE I VALUE 0.

  " Capture ALV changes
  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->CHECK_CHANGED_DATA.

  LOOP AT LT_LIKP INTO LS_LIKP.

    " Skip empty header rows
    IF LS_LIKP-VBELN IS INITIAL
    AND LS_LIKP-ERNAM IS INITIAL
    AND LS_LIKP-ERDAT IS INITIAL
    AND LS_LIKP-VSTEL IS INITIAL
    AND LS_LIKP-VKORG IS INITIAL.

      LV_SKIPPED = LV_SKIPPED + 1.
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    " Keep non-empty row for redisplay
    LS_LIKP_CLEAN = LS_LIKP.
    APPEND LS_LIKP_CLEAN TO LT_LIKP_CLEAN.

    " Prepare structure for DB insert/update
    CLEAR LS_DB_ALV_FLAT.
    MOVE-CORRESPONDING LS_LIKP TO LS_DB_ALV_FLAT.

    " Check if record exists
    SELECT SINGLE VBELN
      INTO @LV_VBELN
      FROM ZDB_EDIT_ALV
      WHERE VBELN = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VBELN.

    IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
      " Record exists -> UPDATE
      UPDATE ZDB_EDIT_ALV SET
        ERNAM = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-ERNAM,
        ERDAT = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-ERDAT,
        VSTEL = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VSTEL,
        VKORG = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VKORG
      WHERE VBELN = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VBELN.
    ELSE.
      " Record does not exist -> INSERT
      INSERT ZDB_EDIT_ALV FROM LS_DB_ALV_FLAT.
    ENDIF.

    LV_SAVED_ROWS = LV_SAVED_ROWS + 1.

  ENDLOOP.
  " Replace the old internal table with the cleaned one (remove empty rows)
  LT_LIKP = LT_LIKP_CLEAN.
  COMMIT WORK.
*ENDIF.
 " Message after save
IF LV_SKIPPED > 0.
  MESSAGE |{ LV_SKIPPED } empty header record(s) were not saved and removed.| TYPE 'I'.
ELSEIF LV_SAVED_ROWS < 0.
  MESSAGE | { LV_SAVED_ROWS } without editing or creating anything were data not saved.| TYPE 'I'.
ELSE.
  MESSAGE 'Header data saved successfully' TYPE 'S'.
ENDIF.
  " Lock the grid after save
  PERFORM DISABLE_ALL_CELLS_IN_HEADER.

  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->SET_READY_FOR_INPUT
    EXPORTING I_READY_FOR_INPUT = 0.

  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY.
ENDFORM.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  UPDATE_HEADER_DATA
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM UPDATE_HEADER_DATA .
  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->CHECK_CHANGED_DATA.
  LOOP AT LT_LIKP INTO LS_LIKP.
    " Map to flat structure (exclude CELLTAB)
    LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VBELN = LS_LIKP-VBELN.
    LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-ERNAM = LS_LIKP-ERNAM.
    LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-ERDAT = LS_LIKP-ERDAT.
    LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VSTEL = LS_LIKP-VSTEL.
    LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VKORG = LS_LIKP-VKORG.
    " Check if record exists
    SELECT SINGLE VBELN
  INTO @DATA(LV_VBELN)
  FROM ZDB_EDIT_ALV
  WHERE VBELN = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VBELN
    AND ERNAM = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-ERNAM.
    IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
      " Record exists -> UPDATE
      UPDATE ZDB_EDIT_ALV SET
        ERDAT = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-ERDAT,
        VSTEL = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VSTEL,
        VKORG = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VKORG
      WHERE VBELN = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-VBELN AND ERNAM = @LS_DB_ALV_FLAT-ERNAM.
    ELSE.
      " Record does not exist -> INSERT
      INSERT ZDB_EDIT_ALV FROM LS_DB_ALV_FLAT.
    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.
  COMMIT WORK.
  MESSAGE 'Header data Updated successfully' TYPE 'S'.

  "  Make all fields non-editable after save
  PERFORM DISABLE_ALL_CELLS_IN_HEADER.

  "  Set ALV to non-edit mode
  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->SET_READY_FOR_INPUT
    EXPORTING I_READY_FOR_INPUT = 0.

  "  Refresh ALV display
  CALL METHOD O_GRID1->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY.

ENDFORM.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  CHANGE_ITEMS_MODE
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM CHANGE_ITEMS_MODE .
   DATA : LT_INDEX_ROWS TYPE LVC_T_ROW,
         LT_ROW_NO     TYPE LVC_T_ROID,
         LS_ROW_NO     TYPE LVC_S_ROID,
         LS_STYLEROW   TYPE LVC_S_STYL,
         LT_STYLEROW   TYPE TABLE OF LVC_S_STYL.

  CALL METHOD O_GRID2->GET_SELECTED_ROWS
    IMPORTING
*     et_index_rows = lt_index_rows
      ET_ROW_NO = LT_ROW_NO.

  IF NOT LT_ROW_NO IS INITIAL.

    LOOP AT LT_LIPS INTO LS_LIPS.

      READ TABLE LT_ROW_NO INTO LS_ROW_NO WITH KEY ROW_ID = SY-TABIX.
      IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VBELN'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'POSNR'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'MATNR'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'WERKS'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        CLEAR: LS_LIPS-CELLTAB.
        INSERT LINES OF LT_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LS_LIPS-CELLTAB.
        MODIFY LT_LIPS FROM LS_LIPS TRANSPORTING CELLTAB.
        CLEAR: LT_STYLEROW.
      ELSE.
        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VBELN'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'POSNR'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'MATNR'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        LS_STYLEROW-STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
        LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'WERKS'.
        INSERT LS_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LT_STYLEROW.

        CLEAR: LS_LIPS-CELLTAB.
        INSERT LINES OF LT_STYLEROW INTO TABLE LS_LIPS-CELLTAB.
        MODIFY LT_LIPS FROM LS_LIPS TRANSPORTING CELLTAB.
        CLEAR: LT_STYLEROW.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDIF.

  CLEAR LT_ROW_NO.
  CLEAR LS_ROW_NO.

  IF O_GRID2->IS_READY_FOR_INPUT( ) EQ 0.
    CALL METHOD O_GRID2->SET_READY_FOR_INPUT
      EXPORTING
        I_READY_FOR_INPUT = 1.

  ELSE.
    CALL METHOD O_GRID2->SET_READY_FOR_INPUT
      EXPORTING
        I_READY_FOR_INPUT = 0.
  ENDIF.
  O_GRID2->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY( ).

ENDFORM.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  CREATE_ITEMS_DATA
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------
FORM CREATE_ITEMS_DATA .
   DATA: LS_NEW        TYPE T_ZDB_EDIT_ALV1,
        LT_STYLEROW   TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF LVC_S_STYL WITH DEFAULT KEY,
        LS_STYLEROW   TYPE LVC_S_STYL.

  CLEAR: LS_NEW, LT_STYLEROW.

  " Define editable fields

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VBELN'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'POSNR'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

   LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'MATNR'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'WERKS'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  " Assign celltab to new row
  LS_NEW-CELLTAB = LT_STYLEROW.

  " Insert new row at the beginning
  APPEND LS_NEW TO LT_LIPS.

  " Set grid to editable mode
  CALL METHOD O_GRID2->SET_READY_FOR_INPUT
    EXPORTING
      I_READY_FOR_INPUT = 1.

  " Refresh ALV to reflect changes
  CALL METHOD O_GRID2->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY.

ENDFORM.

TYPES: BEGIN OF TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV1_FLAT,
         VBELN TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1-VBELN,
         POSNR TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1-POSNR,
         ERNAM TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1-ERNAM,
         MATNR TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1-MATNR,
         WERKS TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1-WERKS,
       END OF TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV1_FLAT.

DATA: LS_DB_ALV1 TYPE TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV1_FLAT.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  SAVE_ITEMS_DATA
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------
FORM SAVE_ITEMS_DATA .
DATA: LS_DB_ALV1     TYPE TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV1_FLAT,
      LS_DB_FULL     TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1,
      LV_VBELN       TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1-VBELN,
      LV_SKIPPED     TYPE I VALUE 0,
      LT_LIPS_CLEAN  TYPE TABLE OF T_ZDB_EDIT_ALV1,
      LS_LIPS_CLEAN  TYPE T_ZDB_EDIT_ALV1,
      LV_SAVED_ROWS  TYPE I VALUE 0,
      LV_HEADER_CHECK TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV-VBELN.

CALL METHOD O_GRID2->CHECK_CHANGED_DATA.

LOOP AT LT_LIPS INTO LS_LIPS.

  " Skip empty item rows
  IF LS_LIPS-VBELN IS INITIAL
  AND LS_LIPS-POSNR IS INITIAL
  AND LS_LIPS-ERNAM IS INITIAL
  AND LS_LIPS-MATNR IS INITIAL
  AND LS_LIPS-WERKS IS INITIAL.
    LV_SKIPPED = LV_SKIPPED + 1.
    CONTINUE.
  ENDIF.

  " # Check if header exists
  SELECT SINGLE VBELN
    INTO @LV_HEADER_CHECK
    FROM ZDB_EDIT_ALV
    WHERE VBELN = @LS_LIPS-VBELN AND ERNAM = @LS_LIPS-ERNAM.

  IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
    LV_SKIPPED = LV_SKIPPED + 1.
    CONTINUE. " Don't allow saving item without header
  ENDIF.

  " Keep non-empty row
  LS_LIPS_CLEAN = LS_LIPS.
  APPEND LS_LIPS_CLEAN TO LT_LIPS_CLEAN.

  " Prepare flat structure
  CLEAR LS_DB_ALV1.
  MOVE-CORRESPONDING LS_LIPS TO LS_DB_ALV1.

  " Check if item record exists
  SELECT SINGLE VBELN
    INTO @LV_VBELN
    FROM ZDB_EDIT_ALV1
    WHERE VBELN = @LS_DB_ALV1-VBELN
      AND POSNR = @LS_DB_ALV1-POSNR
      AND ERNAM = @LS_DB_ALV1-ERNAM.

  IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
    " UPDATE
    UPDATE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1 SET
      MATNR = @LS_DB_ALV1-MATNR,
      WERKS = @LS_DB_ALV1-WERKS
    WHERE VBELN = @LS_DB_ALV1-VBELN
      AND POSNR = @LS_DB_ALV1-POSNR
      AND ERNAM = @LS_DB_ALV1-ERNAM.
  ELSE.
    " INSERT
    CLEAR LS_DB_FULL.
    MOVE-CORRESPONDING LS_DB_ALV1 TO LS_DB_FULL.
    INSERT ZDB_EDIT_ALV1 FROM LS_DB_FULL.
  ENDIF.

  LV_SAVED_ROWS = LV_SAVED_ROWS + 1.

ENDLOOP.

LT_LIPS = LT_LIPS_CLEAN.

COMMIT WORK.

"  Final messaging
IF LV_SAVED_ROWS > 0.
  MESSAGE 'Item data saved successfully' TYPE 'S'.
ELSEIF LV_SKIPPED > 0.
  MESSAGE |{ LV_SKIPPED } item row(s) were skipped – empty or missing header.| TYPE 'I'.
ELSE.
  MESSAGE 'No item data saved.' TYPE 'I'.
ENDIF.



  " Make all fields non-editable after save
  PERFORM DISABLE_ALL_CELLS_IN_ITEMS.

  " Set ALV to non-edit mode
  CALL METHOD O_GRID2->SET_READY_FOR_INPUT
    EXPORTING I_READY_FOR_INPUT = 0.

  " Refresh ALV display
  CALL METHOD O_GRID2->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY.

ENDFORM.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  UPDATE_ITEMS_DATA
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM UPDATE_ITEMS_DATA .

  DATA: LS_DB_ALV1  TYPE TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV1_FLAT,
        LS_DB_FULL  TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1,
        LV_VBELN    TYPE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1-VBELN.

" Ensure ALV changes are captured
  CALL METHOD O_GRID2->CHECK_CHANGED_DATA.

  LOOP AT LT_LIPS INTO LS_LIPS.

    " Map to flat structure (exclude CELLTAB)
    CLEAR LS_DB_ALV1.
    LS_DB_ALV1-VBELN = LS_LIPS-VBELN.
    LS_DB_ALV1-POSNR = LS_LIPS-POSNR.
    LS_DB_ALV1-ERNAM = LS_LIPS-ERNAM.
    LS_DB_ALV1-MATNR = LS_LIPS-MATNR.
    LS_DB_ALV1-WERKS = LS_LIPS-WERKS.

    " Check if record exists in DB
    SELECT SINGLE VBELN
      INTO @S_VBELN
      FROM ZDB_EDIT_ALV1
      WHERE VBELN = @LS_DB_ALV1-VBELN
        AND POSNR = @LS_DB_ALV1-POSNR AND ERNAM = @LS_DB_ALV1-ERNAM.

    IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
      " Record exists -> UPDATE
      UPDATE ZDB_EDIT_ALV1 SET
        MATNR = @LS_DB_ALV1-MATNR,
        WERKS = @LS_DB_ALV1-WERKS
        WHERE VBELN = @LS_DB_ALV1-VBELN
          AND POSNR = @LS_DB_ALV1-POSNR AND ERNAM = @LS_DB_ALV1-ERNAM.
    ELSE.
      " Record does not exist -> INSERT
      CLEAR LS_DB_FULL.
      MOVE-CORRESPONDING LS_DB_ALV1 TO LS_DB_FULL.
      INSERT ZDB_EDIT_ALV1 FROM LS_DB_FULL.
    ENDIF.

  ENDLOOP.

  COMMIT WORK.
  IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
 MESSAGE 'Item data saved successfully' TYPE 'S'.
ELSE.
  MESSAGE 'Created new row but empty record not saved' type 'I'.
ENDIF.

  "  Make all fields non-editable after save
  PERFORM DISABLE_ALL_CELLS_IN_ITEMS.

  "  Set ALV to non-edit mode
  CALL METHOD O_GRID2->SET_READY_FOR_INPUT
    EXPORTING I_READY_FOR_INPUT = 0.

  "  Refresh ALV display
  CALL METHOD O_GRID2->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY.

ENDFORM.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  DELETE_ITEMS_DATA
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
*  -->  p1        text
*  <--  p2        text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM DELETE_ITEMS_DATA .

  DATA: LT_ROWS TYPE LVC_T_ROW,
      LS_ROW  TYPE LVC_S_ROW,
      LV_IDX  TYPE SY-TABIX.

CALL METHOD O_GRID2->GET_SELECTED_ROWS
  IMPORTING ET_INDEX_ROWS = LT_ROWS.

LOOP AT LT_ROWS INTO LS_ROW.
  LV_IDX = LS_ROW-INDEX.
  READ TABLE LT_LIPS INDEX LV_IDX INTO LS_LIPS.
  IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
    " Corrected DELETE to use both VBELN and POSNR
    DELETE FROM ZDB_EDIT_ALV1 WHERE VBELN = LS_LIPS-VBELN AND POSNR = LS_LIPS-POSNR AND ERNAM = LS_LIPS-ERNAM.
    DELETE LT_LIPS INDEX LV_IDX.
  ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.

CALL METHOD O_GRID2->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY.
COMMIT WORK.

MESSAGE 'Selected item(s) deleted' TYPE 'S'.

ENDFORM.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  DISABLE_ALL_CELLS_IN_HEADER
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM DISABLE_ALL_CELLS_IN_HEADER .

  DATA:  LT_STYLEROW   TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF LVC_S_STYL WITH DEFAULT KEY,
        LS_STYLEROW   TYPE LVC_S_STYL.

  LOOP AT LT_LIKP INTO LS_LIKP.

    CLEAR LT_STYLEROW.

    LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VBELN'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERDAT'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VSTEL'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VKORG'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

    " Apply to current row
    LS_LIKP-CELLTAB = LT_STYLEROW.
    MODIFY LT_LIKP FROM LS_LIKP TRANSPORTING CELLTAB.

    ENDLOOP.

ENDFORM.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  DISABLE_ALL_CELLS_IN_ITEMS
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM DISABLE_ALL_CELLS_IN_ITEMS .

  DATA:  LT_STYLEROW   TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF LVC_S_STYL WITH DEFAULT KEY,
        LS_STYLEROW   TYPE LVC_S_STYL.

  LOOP AT LT_LIPS INTO LS_LIPS.

    CLEAR LT_STYLEROW.

    LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'VBELN'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'POSNR'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.


  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'MATNR'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.

  LS_STYLEROW-FIELDNAME = 'WERKS'.
  LS_STYLEROW-STYLE     = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED.
  APPEND LS_STYLEROW TO LT_STYLEROW.


    " Apply to current row
    LS_LIKP-CELLTAB = LT_STYLEROW.
    MODIFY LT_LIPS FROM LS_LIPS TRANSPORTING CELLTAB.

    ENDLOOP.

ENDFORM.

entry.title: PREDICTOKEN: Fast, Local Token Count Prediction for ABAP and Edge-Devices
entry.content:
Hello, my name is Alice, and I've been working with ABAP/SAP for about two decades with a lifelong passion for performance optimization. Today I want to share a technical solution I developed about a year ago when LLM API costs were significantly higher and token prediction was even more crucial than today.

Let me show you 😃

TL;DR:

GitHub: oisee/zllm (see ZCL_LLM_00_PREDICTOKEN)
The Problem: How to predict token counts without calling expensive tokenizer APIs
The Solution: Linear regression trained on ABAP corpus, generating pure ABAP prediction code
The Innovation: Machine learning model deployment in environments with zero native ML libraries
Use Cases: SAP/ABAP systems, edge AI, air-gapped environments, cost-sensitive pipelines

What Problem Are We Trying to Solve?

When building LLM integrations, especially in enterprise environments, you constantly face this dilemma:

"How many tokens will this text consume?"

The traditional approaches all have significant drawbacks:

  1. Call the tokenizer API first - Adds 200-500ms latency + network overhead + additional cost
  2. Conservative estimation - Wastes 30-50% of available context window
  3. Character-based approximation - Wildly inaccurate (can be off by 40%+)
  4. Send and pray - Results in 10-15% failed requests and wasted API calls

 

This was especially painful ~1 year ago when:

  • LLM API costs were significantly higher
  • Every failed request due to token limits was expensive
  • Context windows were smaller, making efficiency crucial
  • Enterprise budgets for AI experimentation were tighter

For ABAP environments, this problem is amplified because there are no native tokenizer libraries. You can't just pip install tiktoken - you're stuck with external API calls for everything. (Except Vector Search: oisee/zvdb 😃

Why This Matters Beyond ABAP

While I built this for SAP systems, the core challenge applies everywhere:

Edge AI devices - Limited connectivity, need local token prediction
Cost-sensitive environments - Every API call matters
Real-time systems - Can't afford tokenizer API latency
Air-gapped systems - No external API access allowed
Legacy environments - No modern ML libraries available

The technique is portable. As long as your environment supports basic string operations and math, this can be deployed without ML runtimes or external APIs.

The fundamental question: Can you predict token counts using only basic math and text processing?

Validation: GOOD ENOUGH

The practicality of PREDICTOKEN is validated based on these criteria:

Accuracy: Prediction error should be < 10% for typical text (< 5% for code)
Performance: Prediction time should be negligible (< 1ms)
Overestimation Bias: Slight intentional overestimation (~2-3%) is built-in to avoid payload cutoff errors
Zero Dependencies: Must work in any environment with basic arithmetic

These criteria ensure the system provides practical value without requiring external services or complex dependencies.

Understanding the Data

Let's examine what we're predicting:

Input: Any text (ABAP code, documentation, mixed content)
Output: Token count for specific LLM tokenizers (GPT, Mistral, etc.)
Challenge: Different tokenizers produce different counts for identical input

Example with ABAP code:

" This ABAP code snippet:
CLASS lcl_ DEFINITION DEFERRED.

CLASS zcl_llm_00_predictoken DEFINITION LOCAL FRIENDS lcl_.

CLASS lcl_ DEFINITION FOR TESTING
 RISK LEVEL HARMLESS
 DURATION SHORT.
 PRIVATE SECTION.
 TYPES: string_t TYPE zif_llm_00_types=>string_t.
 DATA: mo_cut TYPE REF TO zcl_llm_00_predictoken.
 CLASS-DATA: gs_coeff TYPE zcl_llm_00_predictoken=>ts_coeff.
 METHODS: setup,
 test_constructor FOR TESTING,
 test_class_constructor FOR TESTING,
 new_instance_creation FOR TESTING,
 new_for_model_instance FOR TESTING,
 predict_single FOR TESTING,
 predict_for_itab FOR TESTING,
 extract_features FOR TESTING,
 minimal_prediction FOR TESTING.

 DATA: mv_in TYPE string.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS lcl_ IMPLEMENTATION.
 METHOD setup.
 mo_cut = zcl_llm_00_predictoken=>new( ).
 DO 84 TIMES.
 mv_in = mv_in && `This is a test. `.
 ENDDO.
 ENDMETHOD.
 ....
ENDCLASS.

GPT-4 Tokenizer: ~255 tokens
Mistral Tokenizer: ~257 tokens
Character Count: 946 characters
Word Count: 90 words

Simple character/word ratios would give you estimates between 20-40 tokens - not precise enough for reliable payload sizing.

The Machine Learning Approach

Instead of trying to reverse-engineer complex tokenizer algorithms, I took a feature-based regression approach:

Feature Engineering

TYPES:
  BEGIN OF ts_features,
    text_length  TYPE i,     " Total character count
    words        TYPE i,     " Word boundaries (\w+'*\w*|\w)
    punctuations TYPE i,     " [.,;:!?'"] count
    numbers      TYPE i,     " \d count  
    whitespaces  TYPE i,     " \s count
    lines        TYPE i,     " Newline count + 1
    sentences    TYPE i,     " [.!?]\s* count + 1
  END OF ts_features.
 

These features capture the linguistic patterns that correlate with tokenization behavior:

  • Text length: Base correlation with token count
  • Words: Primary tokenization units
  • Punctuation: Often becomes separate tokens
  • Numbers: Special tokenization rules
  • Whitespace: Token boundary indicators
  • Lines: Important for code structure
  • Sentences: Semantic boundaries matter

Training Pipeline

The training happens in Python, but deployment is pure ABAP:

  1. Corpus Collection: Collected diverse ABAP code samples and documentation
  2. Ground Truth Generation: Called actual tokenizer APIs to get true counts
  3. Feature Extraction: Computed the 7 features for each sample
  4. Linear Regression: Trained separate models per tokenizer
  5. Coefficient Export: Generated ABAP constants from Python coefficients

The key insight: Linear regression is simple enough to implement anywhere.

Implementation: From Python Training to ABAP Deployment

Here's the beautiful part. Python training produces these regression coefficients:

# GPT-4 Model Training Results:
# Coefficients: [ 0.0610605   0.5011021   0.93815106  1.16759457 
#                -0.01792212  4.63131677 -1.67103032]
# Intercept: -28.82816309097143
# R²: 0.987 (excellent fit)

# Mistral Model Training Results:
# Coefficients: [ 0.18695786 -0.28615407  0.52599872  1.80894008 
#                -0.02379413  5.65761217  0.34037584]
# Intercept: -56.52809407193536
# R²: 0.981 (excellent fit)
 

Which automatically generate ABAP constants:

METHOD class_constructor.
  " Auto-generated from Python training
  gs_co_gpt = VALUE #(
    text_length  = '0.0610605'
    words        = '0.5011021'
    punctuations = '0.93815106'
    numbers      = '1.16759457'
    whitespaces  = '-0.01792212'
    lines        = '4.63131677'
    sentences    = '-1.67103032'
    intercept    = '-28.82816309097143'
  ).
  
  gs_co_mistral = VALUE #(
    text_length  = '0.18695786'
    words        = '-0.28615407'
    punctuations = '0.52599872'
    numbers      = '1.80894008'
    whitespaces  = '-0.02379413'
    lines        = '5.65761217'
    sentences    = '0.34037584'
    intercept    = '-56.52809407193536'
  ).
ENDMETHOD.
 

Real-Time Prediction: Seven Operations

The actual prediction is remarkably simple:

METHOD predict_tokens.
  DATA lv_sum TYPE decfloat34.
  
  " Linear regression formula
  lv_sum = ceil(
    ms_-intercept +
    ms_-text_length  * is_-text_length +
    ms_-words        * is_-words +
    ms_-punctuations * is_-punctuations +
    ms_-numbers      * is_-numbers +
    ms_-whitespaces  * is_-whitespaces +
    ms_-lines        * is_-lines +
    ms_-sentences    * is_-sentences
  ).
  
  " Ensure minimum reasonable prediction
  rv_ = COND #(
    WHEN lv_sum > mv_min THEN lv_sum
    ELSE mv_min
  ).
ENDMETHOD.
 

Seven multiplications, seven additions, one ceiling operation. That's it.

Execution time: < 1ms on any modern system.

Feature Extraction: The Real Work

The computational work happens in feature extraction:

METHOD extract_features.
  rs_-text_length = strlen( iv_ ).
  
  " Word Count using regex
  DATA word_regex TYPE string VALUE `(\w+'*\w*|\w)`.
  rs_-words = count( val = iv_ regex = word_regex ).
  
  " Punctuation Count  
  DATA punc_regex TYPE string VALUE `[.,;:!?'"]`.
  rs_-punctuations = count( val = iv_ regex = punc_regex ).
  
  " Number Count
  DATA number_regex TYPE string VALUE `\d`.
  rs_-numbers = count( val = iv_ regex = number_regex ).
  
  " Whitespace Count
  DATA whitespace_regex TYPE string VALUE `\s`.
  rs_-whitespaces = count( val = iv_ regex = whitespace_regex ).
  
  " Line count
  rs_-lines = count( val = iv_ sub = cl_abap_char_utilities=>newline ) + 1.
  
  " Sentence Count
  DATA sentence_regex TYPE string VALUE `(\.s*|\?s*|!s*)`.
  rs_-sentences = count( val = iv_ regex = sentence_regex ) + 1.
ENDMETHOD.
 

Even this is fast - regex operations complete in microseconds for typical text sizes.

Why Linear Regression Works

You might wonder: "Why not use a neural network or transformer?"

For token count prediction, linear regression has unique advantages:

  1. Deployable Anywhere: No ML runtime required
  2. Interpretable: You can see which features matter most
  3. Fast: Constant-time prediction regardless of text length
  4. Reliable: No black-box failures or edge cases
  5. Small: Seven coefficients vs millions of parameters

The R² values (0.987 for GPT, 0.981 for Mistral) show that linear relationships capture most of the variance in token counts. While not perfect on edge cases (e.g., emojis, code-mixed text), it achieves excellent accuracy on structured and typical enterprise inputs like ABAP and documentation.

Model Adaptation Strategy

The system supports multiple tokenizers through a simple factory pattern:

" Get predictor for specific model
DATA(lo_predictor) = zcl_llm_00_predictoken=>new_for_model_type( 
  iv_ = zcl_llm_00_predictoken=>gc_llm_type-gpt 
).

" Or auto-detect from LLM configuration  
DATA(lo_predictor) = zcl_llm_00_predictoken=>new_for_llm( io_llm ).

" Make prediction
DATA(lv_tokens) = lo_predictor->predict( lv_text ).
 

Adding support for new tokenizers requires:

  1. Collect training samples
  2. Run Python regression training
  3. Add coefficients to ABAP constants
  4. Update factory method

Practical Applications

This approach enables several enterprise AI patterns:

Smart Context Management: Predict if additional context will fit
Batch Optimization: Group requests optimally without exceeding limits
Cost Estimation: Calculate API costs before making calls
Fallback Strategies: Switch to smaller models when content is too large
Performance Optimization: Avoid expensive tokenizer API calls

For edge AI or resource-constrained environments, this pattern could be even more valuable than in traditional cloud setups.

Stats and Performance

Accuracy:

  • GPT models: < 5% error on 95% of samples
  • Mistral models: < 7% error on 95% of samples
  • Overestimation bias: 2-3% (intentional safety margin)

Performance:

  • Feature extraction: ~10-50μs (depending on text size)
  • Prediction calculation: ~1μs
  • Total overhead: Negligible compared to any network operation

Memory footprint:

  • Model coefficients: ~200 bytes
  • Runtime overhead: Minimal (no caching needed)

Future Enhancements

The framework could be extended for:

Multi-language support: Train on different programming languages
Domain-specific models: Optimize for documentation vs code vs data
Dynamic retraining: Update coefficients based on new tokenizer versions
Ensemble methods: Combine multiple predictors for better accuracy

Why This Matters

PREDICTOKEN demonstrates a broader principle: You don't always need complex ML infrastructure to solve ML problems.

Sometimes the right answer is:

  • Train sophisticated models where you have resources
  • Deploy simple models where you need reliability
  • Bridge the gap with smart feature engineering

For enterprise environments with strict dependencies, air-gapped systems, or edge devices, this pattern of "train complex, deploy simple" could unlock many AI capabilities that would otherwise be impossible.

Conclusion

PREDICTOKEN shows that effective AI integration doesn't require cutting-edge infrastructure everywhere. By training regression models offline and deploying them as simple arithmetic, you can bring intelligent token prediction to any environment.

Whether you're working with ABAP, embedded systems, legacy platforms, or just want to minimize API dependencies, the principle remains: smart feature engineering + simple models can solve real problems.

While this module is part of the larger ZLLM framework (to be released), it's designed to run standalone. The code is available as part of the ZLLM framework. Feel free to adapt the approach for your own tokenizer prediction challenges!

About the Author:

Alice Vinogradova is a Senior Software Engineer with two decades of ABAP/SAP experience and a passion for bridging traditional enterprise systems with modern AI capabilities. She specializes in performance optimization and practical AI integration patterns for conservative enterprise environments.

P.S.

(Brought to you by me in my spare time over weekends, my personal NetWeaver 2022 (Docker) SAP S/4HANA instance, and a couple of imaginary friends: Claude and ChatGPT 😃

This continues my weekend exploration of enterprise AI in ABAP—previously I built ZVDB, a pure ABAP vector database that achieved remarkable performance without external dependencies.

I originally wanted to release ZLLM about a year ago, but it always seemed "not good enough" for public eyes. Despite many weekend attempts to clean it up for release, I kept preferring to "just improve it a bit and add more features"—the classic developer perfectionism trap 😃

So now I've decided to break the cycle: release ZLLM incrementally by submodules in the oisee/zllm repository, gradually filling the gaps. Better to share working solutions than perfect ones that never see daylight!

entry.title: SAP S/4HANAの会計とは？ ~SAP会計領域の特長~
entry.content:
「SAP S/4HANAの会計ってよく聞くけど、一体何なのか？」、このブログをご覧になっている皆様には、そう感じられている方も少なくないかと思います。

SAP S/4HANAとは、SAPが提供するERPシステムです。
ERPであるがゆえ、機能領域が幅広く、具体的なイメージが掴めないという方もいらっしゃるかと思います。

そこで当ブログでは、SAP S/4HANAの会計領域に焦点を絞り、それがどういったものであるのか、また何ができるのかについて、基本的なところをご紹介いたします。
経理部やIT部門でお仕事をされている皆様、またSAPに関わってくださる皆様のお役に立てますと幸いです。

 

目次：
SAP S/4HANAの会計領域の特長

  1. ERPシステムの中の会計機能であること
  2. リアルタイム情報をもとにした分析が可能であること
  3. グループ、グローバルでお使いいただけること

1. ERPシステムの中の会計機能であること

SAP S/4HANAの会計領域の一番の大きな特徴は、ERPシステムの中の会計機能である点です。
会計領域の専門的なパッケージ製品ではなく、SAP S/4HANAという一つのERPシステムの中にある会計機能という位置づけです。
SAP S/4HANAの中には会計機能のほか、購買管理、販売管理、生産管理といった機能が統合されています。そのため、一つの企業の中にある複数の業務部門をまたがった動きを、一つのシステムの中でとらえることができます。

下図はSAP S/4HANAの概要図です。
会計領域の機能のほかにも、複数の業務領域の機能が包含されていることが分かります。


これらの機能は互いに連携し合っています。ここで、製造業を例に考えてみます。
販売部門で受注をします。これを受けて、倉庫にて製品を出庫します。SAP S/4HANAでは、出庫をシステムに登録すると、会計機能にその情報が連携され、「売上原価/製品」の仕訳が自動で登録されます。その後、請求処理を行うと、こちらも会計機能に連携され、「売掛金/売上」の仕訳が自動で登録されます。（売上原価対立法での仕訳処理）


このように、販売部門や倉庫における業務で登録した情報が、そのまま会計仕訳として自動連携されます。
もし双方の部門が別々のシステムを使っていた場合は、それぞれの部門で同じ取引に関する情報を入力することになりますので、企業全体で見ると二重入力をしていることになります。あるいはシステム間のインターフェースを開発して二重入力を避けることも一つの方法ではありますが、その場合はシステム間で不整合が生じていないか、定期的にデータを照合する必要があります。それに対してSAP S/4HANAを使用する場合は、一つのシステムにデータを記録していくため、ある部署で登録された情報は、他の部署で改めて登録する必要はありません。今回の例では、仕訳登録が自動化されますので、経理部が仕訳入力をする必要がなくなっています。
この様子から、SAP S/4HANAは「モノが動けばカネが動く仕組み」とも呼ばれています。

またSAP S/4HANAでは、会計領域としましても下図のように幅広くソリューションを提供しています。
上半分にありますように、財務会計や管理会計といった経理部の方がお使いになる機能のほか、財務部の方にお使いいただける財務資金管理等のトレジャリー系機能、また経営企画部の方にお使いいただける計画や分析の機能を提供しています。
また、左下にありますように、内部監査室の方がお使いになるようなリスクやガバナンス強化のための機能もございます。
さらに右下のように、基本機能と併せてお使いいただくことで業務効率化や自動化を図れるソリューションも提供しています。


SAP S/4HANAでは、会計領域内でもこのような機能間連携のメリットを享受いただけるようになっています。
例として、固定資産管理が挙げられます。
もしSAPではなく、固定資産管理の専門パッケージ製品を使用する場合は、資産情報を専門パッケージで管理し、インターフェースを介して仕訳データを会計システムに連携するという使い方になります。
一方でSAP S/4HANAの場合は、固定資産管理の機能に資産情報を登録すると、それに伴う取引（取得、移動、除売却、償却など）は自動的に会計機能に連携されます。これにより、システム間の整合性が常に担保されるようになっています。

またリース管理、財務資金管理、連結会計の機能につきましても同様のメリットがございます。これらの機能詳細は以下のブログよりご覧いただけます。

■リース管理

  • SAP Contract and Lease Management for SAP S/4HANA～リース会計基準におけるIFRSコンバージェンスへの備え～

■財務資金管理

  • SAP S/4HANAとFinTech融合によるデジタルトレジャリーへの変革　全体概要編
  • SAP S/4HANAとFinTech融合によるデジタルトレジャリーへの変革　第2弾　銀行管理編
  • SAP S/4HANAとFinTech融合によるデジタルトレジャリーへの変革　第3弾　資金繰り管理編
  • SAP S/4HANAとFinTech融合による、デジタルトレジャリーへの変革　第４弾　財務リスク管理編 前編
  • SAP S/4HANAとFinTech融合による、デジタルトレジャリーへの変革　第5弾　財務リスク管理編 後編（為替リスク管理）

■連結会計

  • 連結決算プロセスにイノベーションを！～SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting 連載企画 第1弾(全5回)～　製品コンセプトと特長編
  • 連結決算プロセスにイノベーションを！～SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting 連載企画 第2弾(全5回)～　内部取引照合編
  • 連結決算プロセスにイノベーションを！～SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting 連載企画 第3弾(全5回)～　データ収集編
  • 連結決算プロセスにイノベーションを！～SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting 連載企画 第4弾(全5回)～　連結処理編
  • 連結決算プロセスにイノベーションを！　～SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting 連載企画 第5弾(全5回)～　管理連結とレポーティング編

2. リアルタイム情報をもとにした分析が可能であること

SAPの会計領域の特長の二つ目は、リアルタイムな分析が可能である点です。

こちらの動画はSAP S/4HANAの画面イメージです。ポイントは、常に最新データを分析できるという点です。ダッシュボード画面でグラフを確認したり、アプリのアイコンで重要数値を確認したりできるため、ユーザは対処が必要なものにすぐに気が付くことができます。そしてそこから明細一覧などで詳細を確認し、実際の業務処理を実行するところまで、スムーズに進めていくことができる仕組みになっています。従来はこのような分析処理は、分析専用ツールを利用しないと確認できませんでした。しかしSAP S/4HANAでは標準機能として利用できるため、現状把握から次のアクションまでの流れを分断させることなく実現可能です。


また、先ほど特長の一つ目として、各業務領域の機能と連携ができる点をご紹介しました。この、各業務領域で入力した情報が、管理会計領域にて即座に使用可能な分析軸となることもSAPならではの強みです。
下図は、SAP S/4HANA上で保持される会計データを表現した図です。
一般的な会計システムでは、いわゆる財務会計に用いるデータである、日付、勘定科目、金額、通貨等のデータが保持されるかと思います。一方でSAP S/4HANAでは、それらに加えて、売上高であれば、どの取引先に対して、どの部門で、何が、何個売れたのか、といったような情報まで併せて保持します。そうすることにより、管理会計目的のデータ分析の際に分析軸を改めて付与するのではなく、会計伝票を転記すると同時に、それらの属性情報が管理会計の分析軸として利用可能になります。このため、意思決定の元データとして、データ加工の手間と時間を挟むことなく最新データを常に使用でき、意思決定のサイクルを短縮することが可能になります。

 


実際にSAP S/4HANAでは、こちらの動画のようなデータ分析画面を使用することができます。
現時点までに転記されている会計伝票データを対象に、購買管理、販売管理、生産管理等から引き継がれた分析軸をユーザが自由に組み合わせて分析が可能です。これらの豊富な分析軸が、追加のデータ加工の工数なく利用可能であることは、SAP S/4HANAの大きな特長です。


また、データ分析をサポートする生成AI機能も次々に登場しています。
データ分析を初めて担当する方など、レポートの見どころや分析の仕方が分からない場合、生成AIアシスタントに見どころや取るべき次のアクションを相談し、ヒントを得ることができます。
このようにSAP S/4HANAでは、リアルタイムなデータ分析ができることに加えて、その情報を有効に活用するところまでのサポートを目指しています。


 3. グループ、グローバルでお使いいただけること

SAPの会計領域の三つ目の特長は、グローバルでお使いいただける点です。
SAP S/4HANAは、企業グループの複数社でご利用いただけます。

会計機能の観点では、各社の単体データを保持することはもちろん、勘定科目や組織情報といったマスタの持ち方をグループで標準化することで、法人をまたがった管理会計も可能です。標準化にあたっては、業務改革やマスタコードの標準化などが必要になるため、実現までは決して低くはないハードルがあるかと推察しますが、SAP S/4HANAの機能としてはグループ経営管理を下支えできる仕組みとなっています。

また、複数会計基準にも対応可能です。SAP S/4HANAでは、企業ごとに会計基準に応じた複数の元帳を持つことが可能です。
例えば日本本社ではJGAAP用の元帳とIFRS用の元帳を管理し、同時に北米子会社ではUSGAAP用の元帳とIFRS用の元帳を管理することができます。このように、各拠点にて、ローカル会計基準と全社共通の会計基準の両方で会計データを管理することが可能です。

さらに各国、各地域への対応として、複数言語対応やローカル機能の提供を行っております。
SAP S/4HANAにおけるローカル機能の提供状況につきましては、こちらのSAP Help　Portalにてご確認いただけます。

また、法改正についても随時対応しております。SAPでは、各国、各地域にローカル対応の専門要員を配置しており、法改正を認識した場合にはその対応方法をご案内しています。例えば日本では、これまでにも下図のようなローカル対応を実施しています。


また、その他にも法定レポートや電子インボイス対応といった現地要件に対応するためのソリューションを提供しています。
例えば日本では、消費税申告や支払調書などの法定レポートや、Peppolを用いた電子インボイス対応の機能がお使いいただけます。
一般的にこのようなローカル対応は、要件の洗い出しやシステム対応が現地法人で実施されているケースが多いかと思います。一方でSAPのソリューションを使用する場合は、一つのシステムをグローバル共通基盤とし、その中のローカル機能を各現地法人がそれぞれ使っていくことになります。このため本社にとっては、グループのシステム統制が実現しやすくなります。また本社から各地のデータが確認できるようになることで、業務オペレーションの管理がしやすくなるというメリットもあります。

 


まとめ

ここまでSAPの会計領域について、特長を三点ご紹介しました。

  • ERPシステムの中の会計機能であること
  • リアルタイム情報をもとにした分析が可能であること
  • グループ、グローバルでお使いいただけること

SAPの会計領域は、ある業務領域で使用したデータを会計領域のデータとして自動連携することができるため、入力負荷の低減や照合作業の削減が可能です。また、転記された仕訳データは、すぐに多軸分析が可能になります。これにより、常に最新状況をもとにした意思決定ができます。また、この機能は企業グループ全体でご利用いただくことが可能です。そしてグループの標準化の基盤としてお使いいただきながら、各国各地域のローカル要件にも同時に対応していくことが可能です。

 

最後に、当ブログが皆様のご参考になれば幸いです。
ご要望、ご質問等がございましたら、当社営業など問い合わせ窓口までご連絡いただければと存じます。

entry.title: A journey from Netweaver 7.50 to S/4HANA - Part 3: Hitting some snags
entry.content:
Find part 1 here and part 2 here to learn more about our project in general and "what happened earlier".

Part 3 of my ongoing blog series will focus on how we planned to carry out the manual custom code conversion and how some snags derailed parts of these plans and required some re-planning.

Planning the custom code conversion

Preliminary S/4HANA Readiness Checks via our central ATC system had given us some indication of what all will need to be fixed in our custom code by our team of ABAP developers. While preparing the "cookbooks" mentioned in part 2, we were contemplating dividing the work by simplification items, so that the assigned developer would fix the same issue in many development objects, hopefully gaining momentum while doing so. After some discussions we shelved this plan and decided to divide the work by package, so that a developer would tackle all objects and across simplification items impacted. The main reason for this decision was, to avoid "stepping on each other's toes" and blocking objects with half-baked changes another developer would need to pick up sometime later. Another reason was that we hoped that the work would not be quite as tedious if different simplification items would be in play for a developer while tackling assigned objects.

Getting a list of priority objects in the mix

In parallel to our preparations for the custom code conversion (CCC), the functional and testing teams were running preliminary tests in a converted sandbox system to get an idea of what all no longer worked as it should. As a result of this activity we received a list with objects (programs, function groups and classes) which they deemed priority objects in need of fixing first once we were able to start with the CCC in a converted development system. The list had about 90 objects and we decided to use it for a proof of concept of how to devide up the work and go through the motions of the CCC.

We also decided to tackle and divide this list by main object instead of package to have a simpler relationship between objects and their transport requests. What we want to avoid are dependencies when the time for releasing transports eventually comes, so we opted for (a lot) more but smaller mostly independent transports. We also defined a guideline of how the transport title should look like with a combination of task-ID, fixed keyword (S4HCCC), main object name and initials of the developer working on it. To make it even easier, this concatenated title can be copied from our worklist and should hopefully ensure consistent naming.

We plan to take what we learn from correcting the objects on the priority list and adapt it for the next stage of the project, tackling all the many other objects in need of corrections. Because we have four separate productive systems in play for this conversion, we will prioritize the conversion of objects in the order they are planned to be converted. This for example means that custom code only used in Brazil or in the US doesn't need to be tackled first as they are only relevant for system number 3 to be converted in a few months.

Hitting some snags

SAP's recommendation is to run the S/4HANA Readiness checks directly in the converted system and we already did that with the S4HANA_READINESS_2023 check variant once the latest simplification database had been imported (until you do that you get an error message / tool failure when trying to execute the check). In addition, and while working on objects, we also wanted to make use of the ATC-checks we have been using in our ECC-systems during task release to avoid having "old issues" creep in while fixing S/4HANA issues. As we had the relevant check variant already defined in a central ATC-system (S4HANA 756) I created a new central check combining our task release checks with all the checks active in S4HANA_READINESS_2023.

When I wanted to make use of this check variant, we hit the first snag: a weird error related to the trusted RFC-connection between the checked and the central system which prevented the definition of a check variant via the reference system:

"00024No authorization to log on as a Trusted System (L-RC=1 T-RC=1)."

Our basis team investigated the issue and finally managed to resolve it somehow and I hoped that I would now be able to properly connect the central ATC-system to the S4HANA-Dev system. But, it shouldn't be as now I got a clear error message that our central ATC-system which is on S4HANA 756 cannot provide ATC checks in a system already on 758:

Does the "fun" never end?

Taking a detour to get around these snags

Not being able to make use of our central ATC-system or wait for the system to be upgraded, it became necessary to re-define our main check variant in the S/4HANA development system. In addition, a check variant to define the baseline in that system was also needed to avoid getting inundated in too many unrelated findings. The most tedious task, however, was to adapt the many message priorities based on what we had done in the central system when we set things up there initially. 

Once these settings were in place, I was able to create a local baseline and a default variant which now is the one combining our selected checks with the ones for S/4HANA Readiness. This should make it easy for our development team to execute them often and easily.

Yet another snag

We were however still not "out of the woods" as I noticed that our enhancements - two of which are also on the priority list - were not getting checked as expected but instead produced a tool failure with an error message indicating an inconsistency in the system's namespace settings. I tried setting up check runs for the enhancements but these finished within a few seconds and without doing anything apart from showing the error:

To find out what was happening there, I started a thread in Q&A about "S/4HANA Readiness Check for an enhancement running into "SAP-owned namespace /0SJ1P/ is in scope..." and we were then able to update the entry in table TRNSPACE as needed. When I now open the enhancement via SE84, go to one of the includes and then trigger the ATC-check manually, I'm at least able to get a result. It is however still not possible to include the enhancements in a check run where they are all but ignored from what I can tell, regardless of which objects I add to the selection list.  I have a question about that issue still open here: How to set up an ATC check run for enhancements in an S/4HANA 758 system? 

Apparently, the fun never ends, but this episode certainly does! 

Image generated with Gemini

entry.title: IBM Cloud® Security and Compliance Center Workload Protection for IBM Cloud® VPC and IBM® PowerVS
entry.content:
IBM Cloud® Security and Compliance Center Workload Protection can be used to find and prioritize software vulnerabilities, detect and respond to threats, and manage configurations, permissions, and compliance from source to run. When deploying your architecture in the Cloud, effective protection is crucial for ensuring the health, compliance and early threat detection of your system. 

This feature can be turned on when deploying using the Catalog tiles: IBM® Power Virtual Server with IBM Cloud® VPC landing zone and IBM® Power Virtual Server for SAP HANA®.

Key Features

  •        Real-time visibility: IBM® Power Virtual Server Cloud Security Posture Management. Workload Protection now supports IBM® Power Virtual Server resources as part of its Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) feature. Supported resources includes IBM® Power Virtual Server Workspaces, Instances, Instance Volumes, Networks, Network Security Groups and Public Networks.
  •        Reports: Resource Report generates a CSV that includes Compliance results with the resource detail.

Who is it of interest to?               

IBM Cloud® VPC, IBM® Power Virtual Server and SAP® on IBM® Power Virtual Server Customers 

Benefits

  • Fully automated installation and configuration, add on feature for IBM® Power Virtual Server with IBM Cloud® VPC landing zone and IBM® Power Virtual Server for SAP HANA®,
  • Continuous monitoring of workloads for threats, vulnerabilities, and suspicious behavior.
  • Uses behavioral analysis and threat intelligence to detect attacks in real-time.
  • Simplifies compliance auditing with continuous posture monitoring.
  • Provides reports and evidence for regulatory and internal audits.
  • Centralized dashboard for visibility across multi-cloud and hybrid workloads.
  • Easy management of security policies and incident responses. 

Links

 https://ibm.biz/BdnZKB

 https://ibm.biz/BdnZKx

 https://ibm.biz/BdnZKR

Featured Image Source: IBM

entry.title: The Final Countdown to SAP S/4HANA: Simplify Your Journey with SDT
entry.content:
With the deadline for SAP ECC extended maintenance approaching, customers are planning their transition to SAP S/4HANA to lower the overall maintenance costs of their business solutions. To secure business support, they must build a compelling business case for a transition strategy that is cost-efficient, minimally disruptive, and tailored to their requirements. 

There are three main paths to transition to SAP S/4HANA: System Conversion, Selective Data Transition (SDT), and New Implementation. Some SAP customers rule out System Conversion due to unacceptable downtime requirements caused by the size of their systems, while others rule out New Implementation because it does not support the migration of historical data—only open items and master data. In these cases, Selective Data Transition offers a practical, tailored and effective approach, enabling customers to move to SAP S/4HANA in a way that meets both technical and business requirements.

Using the SAP Readiness Check, customers can assess whether Selective Data Transition (SDT) is the most suitable approach for their SAP S/4HANA journey, based on several key factors:

  1. Pre-Projects and Prerequisites
    Many SAP S/4HANA transitions require prerequisite projects such as New GL Activation or CVI Implementation. With SDT, these can be integrated into the main project. SDT supports "on-the-fly" solutions such as for CVI conversion, Account-to-Ledger mapping (including document splitting), Chart of Accounts conversion, company code merges or splits, system consolidations, and cost center/profit center restructuring—all delivered within the scope of the project.
  2. Downtime Constraints
    For businesses with strict downtime limitations, SDT offers a shell conversion approach. This enables system upgrades—including simplification item handling and custom code remediation—early in the project. As a result, final cutover is limited to data migration and conversion only, significantly reducing the overall downtime window for the business. Performance optimization opportunities during the project can further shorten the downtime window.
  3. System Size and Data Volume
    Large system sizes—often caused by lack of archiving, redundant data from inactive company codes, or uncontrolled growth—can drive up infrastructure and licensing costs in both on-premise and SAP RISE environments. SDT allows customers to selectively reduce data volume (e.g., by time slicing historical data) based on legal and business retention requirements, helping control future system size and cost.
  4. Business Transformation Requirements
    SDT supports broader business transformation needs, such as financial process changes or structural data harmonization. These changes can be embedded directly into the migration, ensuring the new SAP S/4HANA platform reflects current and future business requirements. Using the Landscape Transformation Validation Framework, customers can decide on the number of test cases that they need to validate data between the ECC and their future SAP S/4HANA system. 

The reasons outlined above are key drivers for why SAP customers choose to transition to SAP S/4HANA using the Selective Data Transition (SDT) approach. Many customers also need to phase their global business transitions—due to specific operational requirements—rather than pursue a full 'Big Bang' system conversion. SDT enables this phased approach by allowing transitions by organizational unit (e.g., company code). Selecting this option requires a detailed rollout plan and dedicated resources from both the customer and their partners to ensure the successful and feasible execution of each rollout go-live. 

How does it work? 

The Mix and Match approach is another SDT option that allows SAP customers to begin with a new SAP S/4HANA system, selectively migrating customizations, developments, and only clean data—historical data included if required.”

How does it work? 

The transition to SAP S/4HANA should not be perceived as a technical deadline, but as a strategic opportunity to provide SAP customers with a stronger, future-ready platform that drives end-to-end business transformation and excellence. 

Delivering a Selective Data Transition (SDT) project gives SAP customers the opportunity to implement key business changes as part of the required data migration/conversion, ensuring their future platform is aligned with their strategic business goals.

For support or to discuss your specific business requirements, please don’t hesitate to reach out or contact DMLT directly via their global inbox sap_dmlt_gce@sap.com .

entry.title: SAP Fiori tools 2506 update – Open source, faster app creation, adaptation boost, AI enhancements
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the 2506 release of SAP Fiori tools, bringing enhancements that make full-stack development with SAP Fiori elements and RAP even more seamless—from initial app creation to UI adaptation and low-code configuration. Whether you're just starting your project or looking to fine-tune a production app, this update continues our focus on productivity, extensibility, and user experience.

Let’s take a closer look at what’s new in this release.

SAP Fiori generator: Now fully open source

After a long journey of gradual transition, the SAP Fiori Application Generator is now fully open source. This means all the core logic that powers the generator has been moved to a public repository, giving you full transparency and the opportunity to contribute directly.

If you’re curious about how it works under the hood or want to propose improvements, now is the perfect time to get involved. Learn more in this dedicated blog post.

💡Tip: If you rely on custom generators or extensions, this is a great opportunity to align your tools more closely with the open-source foundation.

Accelerating RAP development with Quick Fiori App

Deploy a ready to use SAP Fiori elements app with ADT, and import it into your dev environment with SAP Fiori tools if tweaks are needed.

To simplify the initial stages of app development, you can now quickly generate a working SAP Fiori elements application based on a RAP business object—directly from ADT. With just a few clicks, a basic app is deployed using the service's annotations, giving you a functional starting point in record time. And when you’re ready to go further, you can import the app into SAP Business Application Studio or Visual Studio Code using SAP Fiori tools, continue customizing, and redeploy as usual.

This feature is available starting with SAP BTP ABAP Environment 2505 and SAP Fiori tools version 1.17.6.

Enhancing adaptation projects with local annotations and more

Use quick actions to open the right adaptation dialog for your task.

UI adaptation projects continue to grow more powerful. This release introduces support for changes that typically involve modifications to the manifest.json—such as creating new pages—now available directly via quick actions in the adaptation editor.

We also added support for creating local annotation files within adaptation projects. Whether you're defining new views or tweaking behavior, you now benefit from the same intelligent editing experience provided by the Language Server Protocol (LSP), including auto-completion, code snippets, and quick fixes—making it easier than ever to stay within the Fiori elements design guidelines.

The outline tree and property panel have also received a usability upgrade, with more quick actions and context menu options—particularly for working within tables.

Smarter tooling and continuous enhancements with AI

The SAP Fiori generator now defaults to using virtual endpoints in newly created projects, simplifying preview configuration and aligning better with modern dev environments.

The AI-powered Project Accelerator continues to evolve as well. It now supports Word documents containing both text and images, giving you even more flexibility when starting a project from design inputs. You can also decide exactly where the generated project should be placed in your workspace, putting you in full control of your file structure.

In the Application Modeler, our low-code editor for SAP Fiori elements, we’ve expanded the configuration options for micro charts. You can now define sort criteria for chart columns and use additional chart types, including “Column,” “Comparison,” and “Harvey.” These changes make it easier to present complex data visually with minimal effort.

Finally, when deploying to Cloud Foundry, you now have the option to select “Application Frontend” deployment within SAP Fiori tools, streamlining the deployment flow even further.

Looking ahead

The future of SAP Fiori tools continues to be shaped by your feedback and evolving development needs. We're working on:

  • Bringing new capabilities to the Project Accelerator, including the ability to extract app information from PDFs, and enhanced Application Modeler’s editing capabilities.
  • Deeper alignment between RAP and SAP Fiori development to further streamline the full-stack development workflow.
  • Better in-tool troubleshooting during development, helping you catch issues earlier.
  • Extended support for SAP Fiori elements features in the Application Modeler for a more complete low-code experience.

As always, we welcome your input and questions in the SAP Fiori elements and tools Community.

We hope these updates help you build great apps faster and more efficiently. Let us know what you think!

If you want to learn about updates to our SAP Fiori tools and technologies, please sign up for the SAP Fiori development newsletter.

entry.title: Task Center ECC Connector – Bridging the Gap During Your S/4HANA Transition
entry.content:
Are ECC-based workflows causing bottlenecks in your S/4HANA migration journey? You're not without a solution.

Introducing the ECC Connector for Task Center, delivered in partnership with MHP, a valuable enabler for customers navigating hybrid SAP landscapes. Whether you're mid-migration or working in a dual-system environment, this connector helps integrate your classic ECC tasks directly into the modern SAP Task Center experience on BTP.

What Is the ECC Connector?

The ECC Connector is a purpose-built solution designed to seamlessly pull tasks from ECC systems into SAP Task Center—your central inbox for all workflow items across SAP and third-party systems.

Built on SAP BTP CAP architecture and deployed directly into your SAP and BTP infrastructure, the connector supports:

  • Pulling ECC-based approval tasks

  • Operating through My Inbox actions (including bulk operations)

  • Detailed task data including attachments and notifications

  • Backend extensibility for customer-specific enhancements

What’s Delivered as an ECC connector?

As part of the implementation, MHP provides:

  • BTP CAP API Interface deployed on your SAP BTP instance

  • Backend Interface in the MHP namespace for ECC/SAP on-premise systems

  • Full implementation support

  • Tailored adjustments to your business requirements

Scope & Eligibility

Your organization qualifies if:

  • You have a BTP subaccount with Task Center and Backend Connectivity enabled (via Cloud Connector & Principal Propagation)

  • You're running My Inbox on ECC for existing approval processes

  • Your SAP system meets minimum version: SAP_BASIS, SAP_ABA, SAP_GWFND ≥ 750

Benefits and Roadmap

Current Capabilities Include:

  • Pulling tasks from ECC backend

  • Support for My Inbox actions (approve/reject)

  • Attachments, details, and multi-backend support

  • Easy display integration for My Inbox detail views

What's Next:

  • Adjustments based on early customer feedback

  • Enhanced support for multiple backend systems, clients, and 3rd party SaaS

  • Full SaaS readiness with 24/7 support, SAP Store listing, and standard rollout models

Why ECC Connector is crutial during S/4HANA Migration?

During your migration to S/4HANA, it's not uncommon to maintain hybrid landscapes where legacy ECC processes coexist with modern SAP BTP components. Instead of re-building workflows or forcing users into multiple inboxes, the ECC Connector provides a unified user experience, reduces disruption, and supports a smoother transition.

ECC Connector in action

<Coming Soon>

Curious how this can work in your landscape?

If you're in the midst of an S/4HANA transition and struggling with disconnected ECC workflows, the ECC Connector for Task Center could be your answer. Reach out to SAP or our partner MHP to join the program and bring clarity and cohesion to your task management.

entry.title: SAP User Feedback Day 2025: Putting Customers and end users at the Center of AI-driven Innovation
entry.content:
Delivering exceptional User Experience is the result of an ever-evolving journey fueled by insights gained from our customers and end users. SAP User Feedback Day 2025 embodied our commitment to listen, learn, and collaboratively shape a future where AI plays a pivotal role in redefining work processes.

AI: The Driving Force Behind Innovation

In today’s fast-paced world, AI is revolutionizing the way we operate—transforming learned patterns and optimizing workflows while empowering humans as the ultimate decision-makers. SAP User Feedback Day embraced this theme, inviting customers and end users to engage in AI-focused dialogues and understand its impact on real-world applications within our products.

On June 17 + 18, 2025, in Walldorf, Germany, SAP brought together end users from seven European customers to explore the transformative power of AI in industries ranging from Automotive to Life Sciences and Private Equity. Our focus was on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, showcasing diverse use cases powered by AI:

  • Fiori First: Manage Sales Documents
  • Batch Release Hub Digital Assistant
  • Joule as a Project Assistant (for Non-Professional Services industry)
  • AI-driven resilient supply chain (IBP)
  • Production Order Release Agent
  • AI Error Resolution

 

Throughout the six specialized use cases and a total of 15 hours of user research being conducted in just the first day, users and SAP experts delved into AI-powered scenarios highlighting AI's role in enhancing productivity and decision-making processes while ensuring human oversight. Employing diverse methods like interviews, usability testing, and moderated discussions, the business users were able to explore how AI can support them in their everyday tasks and provide meaningful feedback crucial for future product iterations.

In addition to the User Research, business users had the opportunity to explore six booths to dive deeper into what AI can do for them and how their feedback shapes SAP’s products.

 

Building a Shared Vision for the Future

SAP’s objective remains the same: empower users with efficient AI tools that enrich their experience and facilitate success. The insights gathered directly influence the alignment of our products with evolving expectations. Engaging face-to-face with end users is an invaluable experience, reflecting our collective journey to tackle today’s challenges and anticipate tomorrow’s solutions. The customers and their end users explored AI's future potential through innovation workshops on the second day of the event, igniting anticipation for forthcoming developments.

The first workshop, "Shape the Next SAP Experience with GenUI," invited key users and business process experts to delve into the next generation of SAP experiences. Participants explored challenges and opportunities within SAP products, offering valuable feedback on GenUI's potential impacts on their roles and organizations. Engaging in brainstorming, foresight exercises, and collaborative discussions, attendees contributed to defining the future of SAP's user experiences.

The second workshop, "Co-creating the Future with AI: AI in Your Company," provided IT experts, in-house consultants, and business process specialists a platform to explore AI's transformative potential in their businesses. This interactive session encouraged participants to share their current AI experiences and identify key opportunities where AI could address pressing challenges. Through collaborative brainstorming, attendees envisioned future states and crafted a "vision journey," discussing actionable steps needed to prepare for this AI-driven future. Both workshops were instrumental in harnessing collective intelligence to drive innovation and enhance user experiences.

Listening and collaborating with our users, we gained many invaluable insights on which we now can start to act. SAP User Feedback Day once again proved our commitment to customers is never fleeting—we pledge ongoing engagement to translate feedback into actionable improvements. The insights drive tangible benefits, refining our products to meet and exceed user aspirations.

Customer Voices

“It was a very good experience to be here. It was a big chance to have the possibility of influencing the products which are not already on the road. It is good to see that SAP values our input. Basically we are partners, we are committed to SAP solutions, we are very satisfied with the performance right know and the better SAP gets, the better we are getting.”

“It was really nice, thank you for inviting us. Really great insights, I learned a lot and got to know lots of people. I’ve seen a lot of features I’m very exciting about – this is the future.”

Collective insights steer our path as we explore AI's immense potential together. SAP remains dedicated to leveraging AI’s capabilities while prioritizing human expertise, crafting products that deliver unmatched value and satisfaction. Join us on this transformative journey toward the future of AI-driven innovation!

entry.title: Introducing the New RISE with SAP Methodology Dashboard: Operations View
entry.content:
What is the Operations View?

The Operations View is a dashboard available for RISE with SAP customers and is designed to deliver daily insights and historical data on system health-related key performance indicators, enabling organizations to efficiently align with the clean core compliance goals of the operations dimension. 

Several metrics are collected from SAP Cloud ALM applications for all your configured and productive SAP S/4HANA systems. These are then evaluated on a daily basis against specific thresholds. The results of these metric compliance calculations are aggregated and organized into KPIs and scores, which can be viewed on the overview and detailed pages of the dashboard.

The metrics, thresholds, and evaluation algorithms are shared to ensure a homogeneous and consistent compliance calculation across all RISE with SAP customers.

 

Key Features and Benefits

The Operations View dashboard offers daily insights and includes up to 30 days of historical data. The data in the dashboard displays the most recent values from yesterday and covers the System Health score and its key performance indicators (KPIs). It offers the following key features and benefits:

  • Overview of the System Health score for all your productive SAP S/4HANA systems (Cloud Private Edition systems and On Premise systems)
  • A system-specific view to identify and analyze metric deviations responsible for each KPI's calculation
  • Jump-ins to standard SAP Cloud ALM applications to access the highest level of details

 

          

 

Concept and Data Model

At the core of the Operations View dashboard stands the definition of Scores, KPIs, and Metrics:

  • Scores
    • A score represents a percentage that describes how well a customer aligns with a specific strategic goal within the operations dimension definition. Examples include ensuring that customer systems maintain good health or verifying that applications belonging to SAP Cloud ALM for Operations are properly set up.
  • KPIs
    • KPIs are distinct objectives that contribute to the strategic goals represented by the score.
    • KPIs are calculated based on the compliance level of each metric.
  • Metrics
    • A metric is a numerical value that offers insight into performance or status. It's often linked to a specific timestamp, such as the number of ABAP dumps (Runtime Errors) from yesterday. 
    • The metrics in this dashboard are retrieved from the Health Monitoring and Integrations & Exceptions Monitoring apps of SAP Cloud ALM.

 

 

All scores (equal to strategic goals) contribute to the Operations Index, an overall percentage that defines the level of a customer's alignment with the clean core compliance goals for the operations dimension.

An Operations Index of 100% indicates a full compliance with the current recommended clean core best practices for the operations dimension. Note that best practices may be enriched and enhanced in future versions by taking into account customer feedback and additional developments.

 

Compliance and Propagation

The Operations View dashboard collects various metrics and calculates a compliance level for each one of them. The result of these calculations are then propagated by aggregating the values into different KPIs and scores. Hence, the metric compliance is at the core of all the displayed values in the dashboard.

How is a metric compliance calculated?

For each metric, we have defined an associated SLA (threshold). The Metric Compliance Calculation is essentially a measure of the distance between an SLA and a Metric value. The result of this difference is then passed to a specific function that returns a percentage value between -100% (minimum level of compliance) and +100% (maximum level of compliance).

 

 

 

 

The dashboard collects daily metrics and calculates the compliance for each SAP S/4HANA productive system*.

Given that the metrics vary in importance for the KPI calculations, each KPI is derived from a weighted average of its contributing metric compliances.

 

A similar approach is used for the calculation of the scores and the Operations Index: each score (strategic goal) is calculated from a weighted average of its KPIs. The overall Operations Index is a composition of all these scores.

 

*The metrics in the Health Monitoring app (SAP Cloud ALM) are related to specific clients. The Operations View dashboard considers only productive clients for its calculations.

 

Current State: System Health, KPIs, and Metrics

The System Health is the first score provided with the release of the Operations View dashboard. It's designed to help customers keep their business-critical systems agile and in good health.

The System Health score is calculated based on the following four KPIs: 

  • Connectivity
  • Exceptions
  • Background Processing
  • Performance

 

To view all scores, KPIs, and metrics that are available in the Operations View dashboard, and to check how much each KPI and metric contributes to the respective value calculations, see At a Glance: Available Scores, KPIs, and Metrics on the SAP Help Portal.

Don't forget that the in-app help can also be used as a source to get more details about the Operations View dashboard and its content.

As mentioned earlier, the dashboard calculates the health of each system. You can view the overall system health (for you selected systems) on the overview page, along with details for each system in the Eligible Systems section.

By clicking on the header of the system tiles, you can access the Operation Details view. Here, a selected system's details along with the metrics responsible for each KPI calculation are shown.

In the Metrics section of the Operation Details view, you can see a list of contributing metrics displayed as charts and cards. These show the latest values from yesterday.

By clicking on a metric card, the dashboard directs you to the standard SAP Cloud ALM applications: Health Monitoring app or Integration & Exception Monitoring app, depending on the metric type.

 

How to Use the Operations View Dashboard?

Please note: it's perfectly acceptable for the overall system health score to be below 100%.

The dashboard acts as a daily report, showing how well customers' systems comply with the clean core principles for the operations dimension. Remember that these principles are expressed through KPIs, metrics, weights, and SLAs used in the respective calculations. Over time, we'll improve these elements.
